original_hypothesis.title: UUPS upgrade surface allows immediate logic replacement of core contracts if owner/timelock can execute upgradeTo
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: EssentialContract inherits UUPSUpgradeable and authorizes upgrades via onlyOwner. If the owner is the timelock (as in deployment) and timelock delay can be bypassed (admin role) or governance is captured, an attacker can deploy a malicious implementation and upgrade critical proxies (TaikoL1, token, verifiers, address managers), enabling arbitrary state changes and asset theft/consensus corruption.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an operational control-plane risk: upgradeability is intended, but combined with timelock bypass or weak governance it becomes a rapid takeover. Validate role assignments and whether upgrades are constrained by additional off-chain controls.
deployment_assumptions: Proxies are deployed for core contracts and owned by timelock/owner as per scripts
Upgrades are executed through timelock schedule/execute
TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE (or equivalent) can reduce effective delay
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol uses UpgradeScript scheduling upgradeTo(address) via timelock; EssentialContract._authorizeUpgrade is onlyOwner.
preconditions: Attacker can cause owner-authorized upgrades (owner compromised / governance capture / timelock admin bypass)
Target contract is behind a UUPS proxy and upgradeTo is callable via proxy
Malicious implementation passes UUPS proxiableUUID checks

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All core components share the same upgrade authority model; compromise cascades across protocol.

operational_questions: Which contracts are deployed behind UUPS proxies in production and who is owner?
Are there policy controls (e.g., multiple independent approvals) beyond on-chain roles?
Are upgrades monitored with canary checks and emergency rollback procedures?
validation_requirements: Enumerate proxy admin/owner for each core contract on deployment
Review timelock role assignments and whether admin bypass exists in practice
Simulate an upgrade and verify what state can be modified post-upgrade (e.g., sweeping TKO bonds)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain ability to execute owner-only calls on the proxy (via timelock/governance)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deploy malicious implementation that preserves storage layout but adds backdoors
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call upgradeTo on proxy via timelock execution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Use backdoor functions to drain assets or corrupt verification/state roots

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Catastrophic: consensus corruption, bridge compromise, asset theft
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All protocol operations controlled by upgraded contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall calls on critical proxies; implementation address changes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected new external methods or event patterns post-upgrade

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
